title: зӢ¬з«ӢжҲҗеҲҶеҲҶжһҗICA
content:
ж–Үз« жқҘиҮӘеҫ®дҝЎе…¬дј—еҸ·вҖңз§‘ж–Үи·ҜвҖқпјҢж¬ўиҝҺе…іжіЁгҖҒдә’еҠЁгҖӮиҪ¬еҸ‘йЎ»жіЁжҳҺеҮәеӨ„гҖӮ

еңЁз»ҹи®ЎеӯҰдёӯпјҢзӢ¬з«ӢжҲҗеҲҶеҲҶжһҗжҲ–зӢ¬з«ӢеҲҶйҮҸеҲҶжһҗпјҲIndependent components analysisпјҢзј©еҶҷпјҡICAпјү жҳҜдёҖз§ҚеҲ©з”Ёз»ҹи®ЎеҺҹзҗҶиҝӣиЎҢи®Ўз®—зҡ„ж–№жі•гҖӮе®ғжҳҜдёҖдёӘзәҝжҖ§еҸҳжҚўгҖӮиҝҷдёӘеҸҳжҚўжҠҠж•°жҚ®жҲ–дҝЎеҸ·еҲҶзҰ»жҲҗз»ҹи®ЎзӢ¬з«Ӣзҡ„йқһй«ҳж–Ҝзҡ„дҝЎеҸ·жәҗзҡ„зәҝжҖ§з»„еҗҲгҖӮзӢ¬з«ӢжҲҗеҲҶеҲҶжһҗжҳҜзӣІдҝЎеҸ·еҲҶзҰ»пјҲBlind source separationпјүзҡ„дёҖз§Қзү№дҫӢгҖӮ

д№ӢеүҚдёҚеӨӘжё…жҘҡиҝҷжҳҜд»Җд№ҲпјҢд»ҠеӨ©зңӢеҲ°гҖҠжңәеҷЁеӯҰд№ е·ҘзЁӢеёҲеҝ…зҹҘзҡ„еҚҒеӨ§з®—жі•гҖӢйҮҢиҝҷдёҖжқЎиҝҳжІЎеҗ¬иҝҮз”ЁиҝҮгҖӮиҖҢзңӢдәҶдёӘеӨ§иҮҙд»Ӣз»ҚпјҢж„ҹи§үе’ҢжҲ‘зҡ„жҜ•и®ҫжңүзӮ№еҘ‘еҗҲпјҢжүҖд»ҘйӣҶдёӯеӯҰд№ дёҖдёӢгҖӮ

0 еҸӮиҖғиө„ж–ҷ

/wiki/Independent_component_analysis

зӢ¬з«ӢжҲҗеҲҶеҲҶжһҗжі•еҹәжң¬еҺҹзҗҶ.pdf

ICAз®—жі•д№Ӣз®—жі•е®һзҺ°

/ica-еҸІдёҠжңҖзӣҙзҷҪзҡ„ICAж•ҷзЁӢ.pdf

FastICA on 2D point clouds

зҷҫеәҰзҷҫз§‘-зӢ¬з«ӢжҲҗеҲҶеҲҶжһҗ

1 з®Җд»Ӣ

In signal processing, independent component analysis (ICA) is a computational method for separating a multivariate signal into additive subcomponents. This is done by assuming that the subcomponents are non-Gaussian signals and that they are statistically independent from each other. ICA is a special case of blind source separation.

дёӯж–ҮдёҚеӨӘеҘҪзҡ„з»ҙеҹәпјҡеңЁз»ҹи®ЎеӯҰдёӯпјҢзӢ¬з«ӢжҲҗеҲҶеҲҶжһҗжҲ–зӢ¬з«ӢеҲҶйҮҸеҲҶжһҗпјҲIndependent components analysisпјҢзј©еҶҷпјҡICAпјү жҳҜдёҖз§ҚеҲ©з”Ёз»ҹи®ЎеҺҹзҗҶиҝӣиЎҢи®Ўз®—зҡ„ж–№жі•гҖӮе®ғжҳҜдёҖдёӘзәҝжҖ§еҸҳжҚўгҖӮиҝҷдёӘеҸҳжҚўжҠҠж•°жҚ®жҲ–дҝЎеҸ·еҲҶзҰ»жҲҗз»ҹи®ЎзӢ¬з«Ӣзҡ„йқһй«ҳж–Ҝзҡ„дҝЎеҸ·жәҗзҡ„зәҝжҖ§з»„еҗҲгҖӮзӢ¬з«ӢжҲҗеҲҶеҲҶжһҗжҳҜзӣІдҝЎеҸ·еҲҶзҰ»пјҲBlind source separationпјүзҡ„дёҖз§Қзү№дҫӢгҖӮ

2 е®ҡд№ү

  • дёҖеҸҘиҜқпјҢд»ҺеӨҡйҖҡйҒ“жөӢйҮҸдёӯеҫ—еҲ°зҡ„жңүиӢҘе№ІзӢ¬з«ӢдҝЎжәҗзәҝжҖ§з»„еҗҲжҲҗзҡ„и§ӮжөӢдҝЎеҸ·дёӯпјҢе°ҶзӢ¬з«ӢжҲҗеҲҶеҲҶи§ЈеҮәжқҘгҖӮ
    • еӣ дёәдё»жҲҗеҲҶеҲҶжһҗеҸӘеҜ№з¬ҰеҗҲй«ҳж–ҜеҲҶеёғзҡ„ж ·жң¬зӮ№жҜ”иҫғжңүж•ҲпјҢжүҖд»ҘICAеҸҜд»ҘзңӢжҲҗжҳҜдё»жҲҗеҲҶеҲҶжһҗдёҺеӣ еӯҗеҲҶжһҗзҡ„е»¶еұ•гҖӮ
  • зӢ¬з«ӢжҲҗеҲҶеҲҶжһҗзҡ„жңҖйҮҚиҰҒзҡ„еҒҮи®ҫе°ұжҳҜдҝЎеҸ·жәҗз»ҹи®ЎзӢ¬з«ӢгҖӮиҝҷдёӘеҒҮи®ҫеңЁеӨ§еӨҡж•°зӣІдҝЎеҸ·еҲҶзҰ»зҡ„жғ…еҶөдёӯз¬ҰеҗҲе®һйҷ…жғ…еҶөгҖӮ
    • еҚідҪҝеҪ“иҜҘеҒҮи®ҫдёҚж»Ўи¶іж—¶пјҢд»Қз„¶еҸҜд»Ҙз”ЁзӢ¬з«ӢжҲҗеҲҶеҲҶжһҗжқҘжҠҠи§ӮеҜҹдҝЎеҸ·з»ҹи®ЎзӢ¬з«ӢеҢ–пјҢд»ҺиҖҢиҝӣдёҖжӯҘеҲҶжһҗж•°жҚ®зҡ„зү№жҖ§гҖӮ
  • зӢ¬з«ӢжҲҗеҲҶеҲҶжһҗзҡ„з»Ҹе…ёй—®йўҳжҳҜвҖңйёЎе°ҫй…’дјҡй—®йўҳвҖқпјҲcocktail party problemпјүгҖӮ
    • иҜҘй—®йўҳжҸҸиҝ°зҡ„жҳҜз»ҷе®ҡж··еҗҲдҝЎеҸ·пјҢеҰӮдҪ•еҲҶзҰ»еҮәйёЎе°ҫй…’дјҡдёӯеҗҢж—¶иҜҙиҜқзҡ„жҜҸдёӘдәәзҡ„зӢ¬з«ӢдҝЎеҸ·гҖӮ
    • An important note to consider is that if $N$ sources are present, at least $N$ observations (e.g. microphones if the observed signal is audio) are needed to recover the original signals. This constitutes the case where the matrix is square ($D=J$ , where $D$ is the number of observed signals and $J$ is the number of source signals hypothesized by the model). Other cases of underdetermined ($DJ$) have been investigated.
      • еҪ“жңү$N$дёӘдҝЎеҸ·жәҗж—¶пјҢйҖҡеёёеҒҮи®ҫи§ӮеҜҹдҝЎеҸ·д№ҹжңү$N$дёӘпјҲдҫӢеҰӮ$N$дёӘйәҰе…ӢйЈҺжҲ–иҖ…еҪ•йҹіжңәпјүгҖӮиҜҘеҒҮи®ҫж„Ҹе‘ізқҖж··еҗҲзҹ©йҳөжҳҜдёӘж–№йҳөпјҢеҚі$J = D$пјҢе…¶дёӯ$D$жҳҜиҫ“е…Ҙж•°жҚ®зҡ„з»ҙж•°пјҢ$J$жҳҜзі»з»ҹжЁЎеһӢзҡ„з»ҙж•°гҖӮеҜ№дәҺ$J < D$е’Ң$J > D$зҡ„жғ…еҶөпјҢеӯҰжңҜз•Ңд№ҹеҲҶеҲ«жңүдёҚеҗҢз ”з©¶гҖӮ
    • зӢ¬з«ӢжҲҗеҲҶеҲҶжһҗе№¶дёҚиғҪе®Ңе…ЁжҒўеӨҚдҝЎеҸ·жәҗзҡ„е…·дҪ“ж•°еҖјпјҢд№ҹдёҚиғҪи§ЈеҮәдҝЎеҸ·жәҗзҡ„жӯЈиҙҹз¬ҰеҸ·гҖҒдҝЎеҸ·зҡ„зә§ж•°жҲ–иҖ…дҝЎеҸ·зҡ„ж•°еҖјиҢғеӣҙгҖӮ

2.1 й—®йўҳеҲҶзұ»

Linear independent component analysis can be divided into noiseless and noisy cases, where noiseless ICA is a special case of noisy ICA. Nonlinear ICA should be considered as a separate case.

2.2 General definition

The data are represented by the observed random vector ${\boldsymbol {x}}=(x{1},\ldots ,x{m})^{T}$ and the hidden components as the random vector ${\boldsymbol {s}}=(s{1},\ldots ,s{n})^{T}.$ The task is to transform the observed data using a linear static transformation ${\boldsymbol {W}}$ as ${\boldsymbol {s}}={\boldsymbol {W}}{\boldsymbol {x}}$, into an observable vector of maximally independent components ${\boldsymbol {s}}$ measured by some function $F(s{1},\ldots ,s{n})$ of independence.

з®ҖеҚ•и®ІпјҢе°ұе°Ҷи§ӮеҜҹеҲ°зҡ„еҗ‘йҮҸйҖҡиҝҮзәҝжҖ§еҸҳжҚўпјҢдҪҝе…¶жң¬иә«зҡ„йҡҗеҗ«жҲҗеҲҶзҡ„зӢ¬з«ӢжҲҗеҲҶиў«еҲҶзҰ»гҖӮ

2.3 Generative model

2.3.1 Linear noiseless ICA

The components $x{i}$ of the observed random vector ${\boldsymbol {x}}=(x{1},\ldots ,x{m})^{T}$ are generated as a sum of the independent components $s{k}$, $k=1,\ldots ,n$:

weighted by the mixing weights $a_{i,k}$.

The same generative model can be written in vector form as $\boldsymbol{x}=\sum{k=1}^{n} s{k} \boldsymbol{a}{k}$, where the observed random vector ${\boldsymbol {x}}$ is represented by the basis vectors $\boldsymbol{a}{k}=\left(\boldsymbol{a}{1, k}, \ldots, \boldsymbol{a}{m, k}\right)^{T}$.

The basis vectors $\boldsymbol{a}{k}$ form the columns of the mixing matrix $\boldsymbol{A}=\left(\boldsymbol{a}{1}, \ldots, \boldsymbol{a}{n}\right)$ and the generative formula can be written as $\boldsymbol{x}=\boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{s}$, where $\boldsymbol{s}=\left(s{1}, \dots, s_{n}\right)^{T}$.

2.3.2 Linear noisy ICA

With the added assumption of zero-mean and uncorrelated Gaussian noise $n \sim N(0, \operatorname{diag}(\Sigma))$, the ICA model takes the form $\boldsymbol{x}=\boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{s}+\boldsymbol{n}$.

2.3.3 Nonlinear ICA

The mixing of the sources does not need to be linear. Using a nonlinear mixing function $f( \cdot| \theta)$ with parameters $\theta$ the nonlinear ICA model is $x=f(s | \theta)+n$.

3 е®һжҲҳ

ж“ҚдҪңдёҠзҡ„ж„ҹи§үжҳҜиҝҷж ·зҡ„пјҢ

еӣҫ/зҷҫеәҰзҷҫз§‘

зӢ¬з«ӢжҲҗеҲҶеҲҶжһҗеӯҳеңЁеҗ„з§ҚдёҚеҗҢе…ҲйӘҢеҒҮе®ҡдёӢзҡ„жұӮи§Јз®—жі•гҖӮиҖҢдё”пјҢз»ҸиҝҮICAеӨ„зҗҶеҗҺпјҢйҮҚе»әзҡ„ж•°жҚ®е№…еәҰеҸҜиғҪеҸ‘з”ҹеҸҳеҢ–пјҢиҮӘиә«д№ҹеҸҜиғҪзҝ»иҪ¬гҖӮ

fasticaжҳҜsklearn.decompositionдёӢзҡ„з®—жі•гҖӮ

Implemented using FastICA: A. Hyvarinen and E. Oja, Independent Component Analysis: Algorithms and Applications, Neural Networks, 13(4-5), 2000, pp. 411-430

еүҚжҸҗпјҡ и§ӮжөӢж•°жҚ®дёәж•°дёӘзӢ¬з«ӢгҖҒйқһй«ҳж–ҜдҝЎеҸ·зҡ„зәҝжҖ§з»„еҗҲгҖӮ

жӯӨеӨ–пјҢзҷҪеҢ–зҡ„ж„ҸжҖқжҳҜпјҢдҪҝз”ЁзҷҪеҢ–зҹ©йҳөж¶ҲйҷӨеҗ„и§ӮжөӢй—ҙзҡ„дәҢйҳ¶зӣёе…іжҖ§гҖӮиҝҷеҸҜд»Ҙжңүж•Ҳең°йҷҚдҪҺй—®йўҳзҡ„еӨҚжқӮеәҰпјҢиҖҢдё”з®—жі•з®ҖеҚ•пјҢз”Ёдј з»ҹзҡ„PCAе°ұеҸҜе®ҢжҲҗгҖӮ

еҜ№дәҺsklearnпјҢжҲ‘зңӢеҲ°дәҶдёӨдёӘеҢ…пјҢ

  • decomposition.FastICA([n_components, вҖҰ]): FastICA: a fast algorithm for Independent Component Analysis.
  • decomposition.fastica(X[, n_components, вҖҰ]): Perform Fast Independent Component Analysis.

е…¶жӯҘйӘӨдёә

дёӢйқўе…ҲзңӢFastICAзҡ„зӨәдҫӢпјҢ

3.2 зҪ‘еҸӢзҡ„fastICA

4 еә”з”Ё

  • optical Imaging of neurons
  • neuronal spike sorting
  • face recognition
  • modelling receptive fields of primary visual neurons
  • predicting stock market prices
  • mobile phone communications
  • color based detection of the ripeness of tomatoes
  • removing artifacts, such as eye blinks, from EEG data.
  • analysis of changes in gene expression over time in single cell RNA-sequencing experiments.
  • studies of the resting state network of the brain.

йғҪзңӢеҲ°иҝҷе„ҝдәҶпјҢдёҚеҰӮе…іжіЁжҜҸж—ҘжҺЁйҖҒзҡ„вҖңз§‘ж–Үи·ҜвҖқгҖҒдә’еҠЁиө·жқҘ~
more:
д№ӢеүҚдёҚеӨӘжё…жҘҡиҝҷжҳҜд»Җд№ҲпјҢд»ҠеӨ©зңӢеҲ°гҖҠжңәеҷЁеӯҰд№ е·ҘзЁӢеёҲеҝ…зҹҘзҡ„еҚҒеӨ§з®—жі•гҖӢйҮҢиҝҷдёҖжқЎиҝҳжІЎеҗ¬иҝҮз”ЁиҝҮгҖӮиҖҢзңӢдәҶдёӘеӨ§иҮҙд»Ӣз»ҚпјҢж„ҹи§үе’ҢжҲ‘зҡ„жҜ•и®ҫжңүзӮ№еҘ‘еҗҲпјҢжүҖд»ҘйӣҶдёӯеӯҰд№ дёҖдёӢгҖӮ

зӢ¬з«ӢжҲҗеҲҶеҲҶжһҗжі•еҹәжң¬еҺҹзҗҶ.pdf

ICAз®—жі•д№Ӣз®—жі•е®һзҺ°

зҷҫеәҰзҷҫз§‘-зӢ¬з«ӢжҲҗеҲҶеҲҶжһҗ

1 з®Җд»Ӣ

In signal processing, independent component analysis (ICA) is a computational method for separating a multivariate signal into additive subcomponents. This is done by assuming that the subcomponents are non-Gaussian signals and that they are statistically independent from each other. ICA is a special case of blind source separation.

дёӯж–ҮдёҚеӨӘеҘҪзҡ„з»ҙеҹәпјҡеңЁз»ҹи®ЎеӯҰдёӯпјҢзӢ¬з«ӢжҲҗеҲҶеҲҶжһҗжҲ–зӢ¬з«ӢеҲҶйҮҸеҲҶжһҗпјҲIndependent components analysisпјҢзј©еҶҷпјҡICAпјү жҳҜдёҖз§ҚеҲ©з”Ёз»ҹи®ЎеҺҹзҗҶиҝӣиЎҢи®Ўз®—зҡ„ж–№жі•гҖӮе®ғжҳҜдёҖдёӘзәҝжҖ§еҸҳжҚўгҖӮиҝҷдёӘеҸҳжҚўжҠҠж•°жҚ®жҲ–дҝЎеҸ·еҲҶзҰ»жҲҗз»ҹи®ЎзӢ¬з«Ӣзҡ„йқһй«ҳж–Ҝзҡ„дҝЎеҸ·жәҗзҡ„зәҝжҖ§з»„еҗҲгҖӮзӢ¬з«ӢжҲҗеҲҶеҲҶжһҗжҳҜзӣІдҝЎеҸ·еҲҶзҰ»пјҲBlind source separationпјүзҡ„дёҖз§Қзү№дҫӢгҖӮ

2 е®ҡд№ү

  • дёҖеҸҘиҜқпјҢд»ҺеӨҡйҖҡйҒ“жөӢйҮҸдёӯеҫ—еҲ°зҡ„жңүиӢҘе№ІзӢ¬з«ӢдҝЎжәҗзәҝжҖ§з»„еҗҲжҲҗзҡ„и§ӮжөӢдҝЎеҸ·дёӯпјҢе°ҶзӢ¬з«ӢжҲҗеҲҶеҲҶи§ЈеҮәжқҘгҖӮ
    • еӣ дёәдё»жҲҗеҲҶеҲҶжһҗеҸӘеҜ№з¬ҰеҗҲй«ҳж–ҜеҲҶеёғзҡ„ж ·жң¬зӮ№жҜ”иҫғжңүж•ҲпјҢжүҖд»ҘICAеҸҜд»ҘзңӢжҲҗжҳҜдё»жҲҗеҲҶеҲҶжһҗдёҺеӣ еӯҗеҲҶжһҗзҡ„е»¶еұ•гҖӮ
  • зӢ¬з«ӢжҲҗеҲҶеҲҶжһҗзҡ„жңҖйҮҚиҰҒзҡ„еҒҮи®ҫе°ұжҳҜдҝЎеҸ·жәҗз»ҹи®ЎзӢ¬з«ӢгҖӮиҝҷдёӘеҒҮи®ҫеңЁеӨ§еӨҡж•°зӣІдҝЎеҸ·еҲҶзҰ»зҡ„жғ…еҶөдёӯз¬ҰеҗҲе®һйҷ…жғ…еҶөгҖӮ
    • еҚідҪҝеҪ“иҜҘеҒҮи®ҫдёҚж»Ўи¶іж—¶пјҢд»Қз„¶еҸҜд»Ҙз”ЁзӢ¬з«ӢжҲҗеҲҶеҲҶжһҗжқҘжҠҠи§ӮеҜҹдҝЎеҸ·з»ҹи®ЎзӢ¬з«ӢеҢ–пјҢд»ҺиҖҢиҝӣдёҖжӯҘеҲҶжһҗж•°жҚ®зҡ„зү№жҖ§гҖӮ
  • зӢ¬з«ӢжҲҗеҲҶеҲҶжһҗзҡ„з»Ҹе…ёй—®йўҳжҳҜвҖңйёЎе°ҫй…’дјҡй—®йўҳвҖқпјҲcocktail party problemпјүгҖӮ
    • иҜҘй—®йўҳжҸҸиҝ°зҡ„жҳҜз»ҷе®ҡж··еҗҲдҝЎеҸ·пјҢеҰӮдҪ•еҲҶзҰ»еҮәйёЎе°ҫй…’дјҡдёӯеҗҢж—¶иҜҙиҜқзҡ„жҜҸдёӘдәәзҡ„зӢ¬з«ӢдҝЎеҸ·гҖӮ
    • An important note to consider is that if $N$ sources are present, at least $N$ observations (e.g. microphones if the observed signal is audio) are needed to recover the original signals. This constitutes the case where the matrix is square ($D=J$ , where $D$ is the number of observed signals and $J$ is the number of source signals hypothesized by the model). Other cases of underdetermined ($DJ$) have been investigated.
      • еҪ“жңү$N$дёӘдҝЎеҸ·жәҗж—¶пјҢйҖҡеёёеҒҮи®ҫи§ӮеҜҹдҝЎеҸ·д№ҹжңү$N$дёӘпјҲдҫӢеҰӮ$N$дёӘйәҰе…ӢйЈҺжҲ–иҖ…еҪ•йҹіжңәпјүгҖӮиҜҘеҒҮи®ҫж„Ҹе‘ізқҖж··еҗҲзҹ©йҳөжҳҜдёӘж–№йҳөпјҢеҚі$J = D$пјҢе…¶дёӯ$D$жҳҜиҫ“е…Ҙж•°жҚ®зҡ„з»ҙж•°пјҢ$J$жҳҜзі»з»ҹжЁЎеһӢзҡ„з»ҙж•°гҖӮеҜ№дәҺ$J < D$е’Ң$J > D$зҡ„жғ…еҶөпјҢеӯҰжңҜз•Ңд№ҹеҲҶеҲ«жңүдёҚеҗҢз ”з©¶гҖӮ
    • зӢ¬з«ӢжҲҗеҲҶеҲҶжһҗе№¶дёҚиғҪе®Ңе…ЁжҒўеӨҚдҝЎеҸ·жәҗзҡ„е…·дҪ“ж•°еҖјпјҢд№ҹдёҚиғҪи§ЈеҮәдҝЎеҸ·жәҗзҡ„жӯЈиҙҹз¬ҰеҸ·гҖҒдҝЎеҸ·зҡ„зә§ж•°жҲ–иҖ…дҝЎеҸ·зҡ„ж•°еҖјиҢғеӣҙгҖӮ

2.1 й—®йўҳеҲҶзұ»

Linear independent component analysis can be divided into noiseless and noisy cases, where noiseless ICA is a special case of noisy ICA. Nonlinear ICA should be considered as a separate case.

2.2 General definition

The data are represented by the observed random vector ${\boldsymbol {x}}=(x{1},\ldots ,x{m})^{T}$ and the hidden components as the random vector ${\boldsymbol {s}}=(s{1},\ldots ,s{n})^{T}.$ The task is to transform the observed data using a linear static transformation ${\boldsymbol {W}}$ as ${\boldsymbol {s}}={\boldsymbol {W}}{\boldsymbol {x}}$, into an observable vector of maximally independent components ${\boldsymbol {s}}$ measured by some function $F(s{1},\ldots ,s{n})$ of independence.

з®ҖеҚ•и®ІпјҢе°ұе°Ҷи§ӮеҜҹеҲ°зҡ„еҗ‘йҮҸйҖҡиҝҮзәҝжҖ§еҸҳжҚўпјҢдҪҝе…¶жң¬иә«зҡ„йҡҗеҗ«жҲҗеҲҶзҡ„зӢ¬з«ӢжҲҗеҲҶиў«еҲҶзҰ»гҖӮ

2.3 Generative model

2.3.1 Linear noiseless ICA

The components $x{i}$ of the observed random vector ${\boldsymbol {x}}=(x{1},\ldots ,x{m})^{T}$ are generated as a sum of the independent components $s{k}$, $k=1,\ldots ,n$:

weighted by the mixing weights $a_{i,k}$.

The same generative model can be written in vector form as $\boldsymbol{x}=\sum{k=1}^{n} s{k} \boldsymbol{a}{k}$, where the observed random vector ${\boldsymbol {x}}$ is represented by the basis vectors $\boldsymbol{a}{k}=\left(\boldsymbol{a}{1, k}, \ldots, \boldsymbol{a}{m, k}\right)^{T}$.

The basis vectors $\boldsymbol{a}{k}$ form the columns of the mixing matrix $\boldsymbol{A}=\left(\boldsymbol{a}{1}, \ldots, \boldsymbol{a}{n}\right)$ and the generative formula can be written as $\boldsymbol{x}=\boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{s}$, where $\boldsymbol{s}=\left(s{1}, \dots, s_{n}\right)^{T}$.

2.3.2 Linear noisy ICA

With the added assumption of zero-mean and uncorrelated Gaussian noise $n \sim N(0, \operatorname{diag}(\Sigma))$, the ICA model takes the form $\boldsymbol{x}=\boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{s}+\boldsymbol{n}$.

2.3.3 Nonlinear ICA

The mixing of the sources does not need to be linear. Using a nonlinear mixing function $f( \cdot| \theta)$ with parameters $\theta$ the nonlinear ICA model is $x=f(s | \theta)+n$.

4 еә”з”Ё

  • optical Imaging of neurons
  • neuronal spike sorting
  • face recognition
  • modelling receptive fields of primary visual neurons
  • predicting stock market prices
  • mobile phone communications
  • color based detection of the ripeness of tomatoes
  • removing artifacts, such as eye blinks, from EEG data.
  • analysis of changes in gene expression over time in single cell RNA-sequencing experiments.
  • studies of the resting state network of the brain.
